title: Projects, Programs, Portfolios, and Operations

eco_mappings.task: Task 1: Define and establish project governance

learning_objectives: Define project, program, portfolio, and operations, and explain how they relate.
Distinguish project work from ongoing operational work using concrete examples.
Explain why portfolio management is about doing the right projects while project management is about doing projects right.

sections.title: Definition
sections.content: A **project** is a temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result. Temporary means it has a defined start and end; unique means the outcome differs in some meaningful way from anything produced before, even if the process resembles past work.

sections.title: Programs vs. Portfolios
sections.content:
A **program** is a group of related projects, subsidiary programs, and program activities managed together to obtain benefits not available from managing them individually. Programs exist because coordinating related work creates value the individual projects couldn't achieve alone.

A **portfolio** is a collection of projects, programs, and operations managed as a group to achieve strategic objectives. Portfolio management is about doing the *right* projects; project management is about doing projects *right*.

sections.title: Operations
sections.content:
**Operations** are ongoing, repetitive work that sustains the business (running a factory line, processing payroll). Projects end; operations continue. A project frequently ends by handing its output into ongoing operations.

A useful way to think about the relationship: a portfolio contains programs and projects aligned to strategy; a program coordinates related projects for shared benefit; a project delivers a specific, bounded outcome; and operations absorb and sustain what the project produced.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Watch for scenarios that test whether you can tell a project apart from operational work (a one-time system migration is a project; the ongoing IT helpdesk that supports it afterward is operations), and whether a described activity belongs at the project, program, or portfolio level based on its scope and strategic framing.

exam_traps: Assuming any large or complex initiative is automatically a 'program' — the defining feature is coordinated related projects for shared benefit, not size alone.
Confusing 'doing projects right' (project management) with 'doing the right projects' (portfolio management) — the exam tests this distinction directly.
Treating a project's handoff to operations as the project's failure rather than its normal, intended conclusion.

flashcards.definition: A temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result.
A group of related projects and activities managed together for benefits not available from managing them individually.
A collection of projects, programs, and operations managed as a group to achieve strategic objectives.
Ongoing, repetitive work that sustains the business, distinct from the temporary nature of a project.

knowledge_checks.question: A company groups a data-center migration, a network upgrade, and a staff retraining effort under one coordinating manager because together they deliver a shared modernization benefit none would achieve alone. What is this grouping?
options: A portfolio, since it contains more than one related project at the same time
An operation, since all of the work supports ongoing company infrastructure
A phase, since the three efforts are scheduled to run one after another
A program, since related projects are coordinated for a shared benefit
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: A program, since related projects are coordinated for a shared benefit
knowledge_checks.explanation: Related projects managed together for a benefit unavailable individually is the definition of a program. A portfolio groups work for strategic alignment, not shared benefit coordination; operations are ongoing rather than temporary; and a phase is a subdivision of a single project.

knowledge_checks.question: Which statement best captures the difference between portfolio management and project management?
options: Portfolios select the right work for strategy; projects deliver that work correctly
Portfolio management simply involves larger budgets than project management does
Portfolio management replaces project management once an organization matures
Portfolios handle the ongoing operations while projects handle business change
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Portfolios select the right work for strategy; projects deliver that work correctly
knowledge_checks.explanation: The classic distinction: portfolios are about doing the right projects, projects are about doing the work right. Budget size isn't the differentiator, neither discipline replaces the other, and portfolios contain projects and programs (and may include operations) rather than dividing change from operations.

knowledge_checks.question: A payroll team processes salaries every month using the same system and procedures. Why is this not a project?
options: It has no dedicated budget or cost baseline of its own
It does not require a certified project manager to run it
It is ongoing and repetitive rather than temporary and unique
It is largely automated, so no active management is needed
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: It is ongoing and repetitive rather than temporary and unique
knowledge_checks.explanation: Projects are temporary and produce unique results; monthly payroll is continuing, repetitive operations. Budgets and management titles don't define project status, and operations still require active management even when partly automated.

title: PMBOK 8 Domains, Focus Areas, and the 40-Process Map

eco_mappings.task: Task 1: Define and establish project governance

learning_objectives: Name and describe the seven PMBOK 8 Performance Domains and the five Focus Areas.
Explain how Performance Domains (the 'what') and Focus Areas (the 'when') form a two-axis structure.
Describe the role of the 40 non-prescriptive processes within that structure.

sections.title: Two Intersecting Axes
sections.content: PMBOK 8 organizes project management along two intersecting axes, and understanding both is foundational to everything else in this course.

sections.title: Performance Domains (the What)
sections.content: **Performance Domains** describe seven areas of practice that matter throughout a project: **Governance, Scope, Schedule, Finance, Stakeholders, Resources, and Risk**. These replaced PMBOK 7's more abstract eight domains, and before that, PMBOK 6's ten knowledge areas — each edition reorganizing the same underlying body of practice.

sections.title: Focus Areas (the When)
sections.content: **Focus Areas** describe five lifecycle groupings: **Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring and Controlling, and Closing**. These are the same five Process Groups long-time practitioners will recognize, reframed as approach-agnostic — they apply whether a project is predictive, agile, or hybrid, not just a waterfall sequence.

sections.title: The 40-Process Map
sections.content: Where the two axes intersect, PMBOK 8 places **40 non-prescriptive processes** — practices with recognizable inputs, tools/techniques, and outputs (ITTOs), but framed as *options to tailor*, not mandatory steps. This is a deliberate middle ground: PMBOK 6 was criticized as too rigid with 49 prescriptive processes; PMBOK 7 was criticized as too abstract with no process detail at all.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: The key skill isn't memorizing all 40 processes — it's recognizing which Performance Domain and Focus Area a described activity belongs to, and understanding that the process itself is a *tailorable option*, not a required checklist step.

exam_traps: Treating the 40 processes as mandatory steps rather than tailorable options.
Confusing Performance Domains (what areas matter) with Focus Areas (when in the lifecycle).
Assuming Focus Areas imply a strict sequential waterfall — they're approach-agnostic and apply to agile and hybrid work too.

flashcards.definition: One of seven areas of practice (Governance, Scope, Schedule, Finance, Stakeholders, Resources, Risk) critical throughout a project.
One of five lifecycle groupings (Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring and Controlling, Closing) — the renamed Process Groups.
A tailorable practice with recognizable ITTOs, offered as an option rather than a mandatory step.

knowledge_checks.question: In PMBOK 8, what do the seven Performance Domains describe?
options: The strict sequence of stages every project must follow
The five lifecycle groupings that organize processes
The mandatory steps required for PMBOK compliance
The areas of practice that matter throughout a project
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The areas of practice that matter throughout a project
knowledge_checks.explanation: Performance Domains (Governance, Scope, Schedule, Finance, Stakeholders, Resources, Risk) are the 'what' — areas of practice relevant across the whole project. The lifecycle groupings are the Focus Areas, not the domains, and nothing in PMBOK 8 is a mandatory sequence or compliance checklist.

knowledge_checks.question: A project manager tailoring an agile project asks whether the 40 PMBOK 8 processes apply to their work. What is the best answer?
options: They are tailorable options, not mandatory steps, across delivery approaches
They apply only to fully predictive projects, never to agile or hybrid ones
All 40 must be performed for the project to claim genuine PMBOK alignment
They were removed from PMBOK 8 for adaptive and hybrid projects entirely
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: They are tailorable options, not mandatory steps, across delivery approaches
knowledge_checks.explanation: The 40 processes are deliberately non-prescriptive — options to tailor to context, applicable to predictive, agile, and hybrid work alike. Treating them as waterfall-only or as a mandatory checklist repeats the rigidity PMBOK 8 was designed to avoid.

knowledge_checks.question: Which pairing correctly describes the two axes of the PMBOK 8 structure?
options: Focus Areas describe the 'what'; Performance Domains describe the 'when'
Both axes describe lifecycle timing at different levels of detail
Performance Domains describe the 'what'; Focus Areas describe the 'when'
Domains apply to agile work while Focus Areas apply to predictive work
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Performance Domains describe the 'what'; Focus Areas describe the 'when'
knowledge_checks.explanation: Domains are areas of practice (the what); Focus Areas are lifecycle groupings (the when). Reversing them is the most common confusion, and neither axis is approach-specific — both apply regardless of delivery method.

title: Project Phases, Deliverables, and Phase Gates

eco_mappings.task: Task 1: Develop an integrated project management plan and plan delivery

learning_objectives: Define project life cycle, phase, and phase gate.
Compare sequential, overlapping, and iterative phase relationships.
Explain the changing cost of change across a project's life cycle and why it justifies phase gates.

sections.title: Life Cycle and Phases
sections.content: A **project life cycle** is the series of phases a project passes through from start to finish. A **phase** is a collection of logically related project activities culminating in the completion of one or more deliverables.

sections.title: Phase Relationships
sections.content:
Phases can be organized in different relationships:
- **Sequential** — one phase finishes before the next starts
- **Overlapping** — a phase starts before the prior one finishes, used to compress schedule
- **Iterative** — phases repeat as understanding deepens, refining the product incrementally

sections.title: Phase Gates
sections.content: A **phase gate** (stage gate, phase review, kill point) is a review at the end of a phase where a decision is made: continue, continue with modifications, end the project, or repeat the phase. Phase gates exist to prevent bad investments from continuing purely on momentum.

sections.title: The Changing Cost of Change
sections.content: Early in a project, changes are relatively cheap because little has been built yet. As the project progresses, the cost of making a change (or fixing a defect) rises sharply. This justifies planning rigor early and using phase gates to catch problems while they're still cheap to fix.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Phase-gate scenarios often test whether you'd recommend continuing a struggling project (sunk cost reasoning is a trap) versus making a governance-based go/no-go call using current data.

exam_traps: Sunk cost reasoning — recommending continuation because of resources already spent, rather than evaluating current data.
Assuming phase gates only exist in predictive projects.
Confusing 'phase' (project-specific, variable count) with 'Focus Area' (fixed, universal five).

flashcards.definition: A review at the end of a phase to decide whether to continue, modify, end, or repeat.
Phases where one finishes before the next starts.
The tendency for the cost of making changes or fixing defects to rise as a project progresses.

knowledge_checks.question: At a phase-gate review, a project is significantly over budget and its business justification has weakened, but it has already consumed 60% of its funding. Sponsors argue it is 'too far along to stop.' What does sound gate discipline require?
options: Continue the project, since the sunk investment already made justifies finishing
Decide based on current data and remaining value, not resources already spent
Skip the gate decision entirely this time, since it has become too contentious
End any project that arrives at a gate while running over its approved budget
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Decide based on current data and remaining value, not resources already spent
knowledge_checks.explanation: Phase gates exist precisely to stop bad investments continuing on momentum. 'Too far along to stop' is sunk cost reasoning — money already spent is gone either way; only remaining cost versus remaining value should drive the decision. Being over budget alone doesn't mandate termination, and skipping the gate abandons governance when it matters most.

knowledge_checks.question: Why does the rising cost of change over a project's life justify early planning rigor and phase gates?
options: Because late changes are always rejected automatically by change control anyway
Because phase gates transfer the cost of late changes onto the vendor instead
Because early project phases have more schedule float available to absorb them
Problems are cheapest to fix early, so gates catch them while change is cheap
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Problems are cheapest to fix early, so gates catch them while change is cheap
knowledge_checks.explanation: The cost of change rises sharply as work accumulates, so catching problems at early gates is far cheaper than discovering them late. Change control evaluates late changes rather than automatically rejecting them, cost isn't transferred by gates, and float is a schedule concept unrelated to the cost-of-change curve.

knowledge_checks.question: A design phase begins before the preceding requirements phase has fully finished, in order to compress the overall schedule. Which phase relationship is this?
options: Overlapping
Sequential
Iterative
Incremental
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Overlapping
knowledge_checks.explanation: Starting a phase before its predecessor finishes is the overlapping relationship, used to compress schedule at the price of rework risk. Sequential phases finish before the next starts; iterative phases repeat as understanding deepens; incremental describes delivering the product in usable pieces, not a phase relationship.

title: The Six Project Management Principles
learning_objectives: List and briefly explain each of the six PMBOK 8 principles.
Apply the principles as a tiebreaker when multiple exam answer choices seem technically defensible.
Explain why principles are decision-making lenses rather than rigid rules.

sections.title: From Twelve to Six
sections.content: PMBOK 8 consolidates project management principles down to **six**, refined from the twelve introduced in PMBOK 7 to reduce overlap and make them easier to apply under exam pressure.

sections.title: The Six Principles
sections.content:
1. **Adopt a Holistic View** — see the project within its broader context.
2. **Focus on Value** — every activity should connect to intended outcomes.
3. **Embed Quality Into Processes and Deliverables** — build quality in rather than inspecting for it later.
4. **Be an Accountable Leader** — take ownership regardless of formal authority.
5. **Integrate Sustainability Within All Project Areas** — consider environmental, social, and economic impact.
6. **Build an Empowered Culture** — foster a team that's trusted to make decisions within its scope.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: These aren't rigid rules — they're decision-making lenses. When multiple options seem plausible, the answer reflecting proactive ownership, value-focus, and empowerment over rigid rule-following is usually the intended one.

exam_traps: Picking the answer that follows a rigid process step over the one reflecting better judgment and value focus.
Treating 'embed quality' as only a Quality-domain concept.
Assuming an empowered team means no accountability — principles 4 and 6 work together.

flashcards.definition: Ensure every project activity contributes to intended outcomes, not just deliverables.
Demonstrating responsibility and ethical conduct regardless of formal authority level.
A team environment where people are trusted and capable of making decisions within their scope.

knowledge_checks.question: Two exam options both seem defensible: one follows a documented process step precisely, the other adapts the step to protect the project's intended outcome. Which principle-based lens usually identifies the intended answer?
options: Focus on value — connect the action to the intended outcome
Follow the documented process step exactly as it is written
Escalate to the sponsor whenever two options seem equally close
Defer to whichever senior stakeholder has stated a preference
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Focus on value — connect the action to the intended outcome
knowledge_checks.explanation: The principles are decision lenses, and value-focus is the most common tiebreaker: the intended answer usually reflects judgment in service of outcomes over rigid rule-following. Escalation and deference to seniority are both patterns the exam tends to punish when the project manager could reasonably decide.

knowledge_checks.question: What does 'Be an Accountable Leader' mean for a project manager who lacks formal authority over the people doing the work?
options: Accountability transfers to whoever actually holds the formal authority instead
Act strictly within formally delegated boundaries and take nothing else on
Escalate every significant decision upward to the level that holds authority
Take ownership of outcomes anyway, leading through influence rather than position
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Take ownership of outcomes anyway, leading through influence rather than position
knowledge_checks.explanation: Accountable leadership is ownership regardless of formal authority — influence, relationship, and initiative fill the gap. Deflecting accountability to the org chart, retreating to formal boundaries, or escalating everything are all abdications of the principle.

knowledge_checks.question: How do the principles 'Be an Accountable Leader' and 'Build an Empowered Culture' work together rather than conflict?
options: Empowerment removes the need for anyone on the team to stay accountable
Accountability requires the leader to personally approve each team decision
The team decides within its scope while the leader still owns the outcomes
They genuinely conflict, and the exam expects you to pick one over the other
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The team decides within its scope while the leader still owns the outcomes
knowledge_checks.explanation: Empowerment and accountability are complements: teams get defined decision space, and the leader remains answerable for results. Approving every decision destroys empowerment, and neither principle cancels the other — treating them as a forced choice is the trap.

title: Project Charter, Business Case, and Benefits Plan

eco_mappings.task: Task 1: Define and establish project governance

learning_objectives: Explain the purpose of the business case, project charter, and benefits management plan.
Describe why charter approval functions as a hard gate before planning begins.
Distinguish delivering a project successfully from realizing its intended benefits.

sections.title: Business Case
sections.content: The **business case** answers whether the project is worth doing at all: it documents the business need, analyzes feasible options, and provides the justification for investing organizational resources.

sections.title: Project Charter
sections.content: The **project charter** formally authorizes the project's existence and gives the project manager authority to apply organizational resources. Without a charter, a project manager has no formal standing to commit resources — this is why charter approval is treated as a hard gate before real planning begins.

sections.title: Benefits Management Plan
sections.content: The **benefits management plan** describes how and when the benefits promised in the business case will actually be delivered and measured — often extending beyond the project's own end date. This plan typically names a benefits owner responsible for tracking realization after the project team has moved on.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Business case justifies investment → charter authorizes the project → project delivers outputs → benefits plan tracks whether those outputs produced the promised value. Exam scenarios test whether you recognize a project can be delivered successfully while still failing to realize its intended benefits.

exam_traps: Equating on-time/on-budget delivery with project success — benefits realization is a separate, later measure.
Assuming the project manager can commit resources before the charter is approved.
Forgetting the benefits management plan's tracking often continues after the project itself has closed.

flashcards.definition: The document justifying the business need and investment for a project.
The document formally authorizing a project and granting the project manager authority to apply resources.
Describes how and when a project's promised benefits will be delivered and measured, including after closure.

knowledge_checks.question: Which document formally authorizes the project's existence and gives the project manager authority to apply organizational resources?
options: The business case
The project charter
The benefits management plan
The integrated project management plan
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The project charter
knowledge_checks.explanation: Authorization is the charter's defining function. The business case justifies the investment but authorizes nothing, the benefits plan tracks value realization after delivery, and the project management plan describes how the work will be managed once the project already exists.

knowledge_checks.question: A project delivered on time and on budget, but a year later the promised efficiency gains never appeared. Which artifact was specifically designed to catch this?
options: The schedule baseline, by comparing actual dates against the plan
The project charter, since it originally authorized the project
The benefits management plan, which tracks realization after delivery
The issue log, which records problems raised during execution
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The benefits management plan, which tracks realization after delivery
knowledge_checks.explanation: Benefits realization is measured after delivery, often after project closure, by the benefits management plan and its named benefits owner. On-time/on-budget delivery is a different measure — a project can meet every constraint and still fail to produce its promised value, which is exactly the gap this artifact exists to expose.

knowledge_checks.question: What is the correct sequence of justification, authorization, delivery, and value tracking?
options: Charter authorizes → business case justifies → benefits plan delivers the outputs
Business case justifies → charter authorizes → project delivers → benefits plan tracks value
Benefits plan justifies → charter tracks value → business case closes the project
The charter and business case are one document reviewed at two different gates
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Business case justifies → charter authorizes → project delivers → benefits plan tracks value
knowledge_checks.explanation: The flow runs from why (business case) to formal existence (charter) to outputs (project) to whether the outputs produced the promised value (benefits plan). The distractors scramble that order or wrongly merge two distinct documents.

title: Integrated Project Management Plan and Baselines

eco_mappings.task: Task 1: Develop an integrated project management plan and plan delivery

learning_objectives: Define the integrated project management plan and its subsidiary plans.
Define scope, schedule, and cost baselines and explain their role in measuring performance.
Explain why internal consistency across subsidiary plans is the core purpose of integration.

sections.title: What the Integrated Plan Is
sections.content: The **project management plan** is the document that describes how the project will be executed, monitored, controlled, and closed. It's not one document but an integrated collection of subsidiary plans (scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, communications, risk, procurement, stakeholder) that must work together coherently.

sections.title: Subsidiary Plans and Baselines
sections.content: A **baseline** is the approved version of a plan component used as a basis for comparing actual performance — most commonly the **scope baseline**, **schedule baseline**, and **cost baseline**. A baseline only changes through formal change control; it isn't simply updated whenever work shifts.

sections.title: Why Integration Matters
sections.content: Integration means the subsidiary plans agree with each other: the schedule reflects the approved scope, the budget reflects the approved schedule, and so on. A discovered inconsistency between subsidiary plans — even one each specialist developed correctly in isolation — needs to be reconciled before the plan is trustworthy.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: Watch for scenarios describing a conflict between two subsidiary plans or between current performance and a baseline. The correct response is almost always to reconcile through the integrated plan and formal change control, not to let one subsidiary plan quietly override another.

exam_traps: Treating subsidiary plans as independent silos rather than parts of one coherent integrated plan.
Confusing a 'plan' (intended approach) with a 'baseline' (the approved reference point performance is measured against).
Assuming a baseline can be informally adjusted to match actual progress rather than only through formal change control.

flashcards.definition: The approved version of a plan component used as a basis for comparing actual performance.
The coherent collection of subsidiary plans describing how a project will be executed, monitored, controlled, and closed.

knowledge_checks.question: How does a baseline differ from a plan?
options: The approved measurement reference, changed only through formal change control
An early draft version of the plan kept for historical comparison purposes
A plan is formally approved, while a baseline always remains informal
A reference that exists only for cost, never for scope or for schedule
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The approved measurement reference, changed only through formal change control
knowledge_checks.explanation: A baseline is the frozen, approved reference point that actual performance is compared against, and it moves only through formal change control. It's the opposite of a draft or informal artifact, and scope, schedule, and cost all carry baselines.

knowledge_checks.question: The schedule assumes a delivery date two weeks earlier than the date the cost baseline was built on. What does integration require?
options: Trusting whichever of the two plans was formally approved first
Letting each specialist keep the assumption their own plan was built on
Reconciling the subsidiary plans so they tell one consistent story
Averaging the two dates as a reasonable compromise
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Reconciling the subsidiary plans so they tell one consistent story
knowledge_checks.explanation: Integration means the subsidiary plans agree with each other — a discovered contradiction gets reconciled through the integrated plan, not resolved by approval order, left to stand, or split down the middle. Approval order says nothing about which assumption is right, and an average satisfies neither plan's logic.

knowledge_checks.question: Actual progress has drifted well behind the schedule baseline, and a team member suggests simply 'updating the baseline to match reality.' What is wrong with that suggestion?
options: Nothing — baselines should continuously track actual progress as it happens
Baselines change only through change control, not to absorb performance drift
Baselines can never be changed at all once they have been formally approved
Only the sponsor is permitted to view or discuss baseline performance data
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Baselines change only through change control, not to absorb performance drift
knowledge_checks.explanation: Quietly moving the baseline to match actuals destroys the reference point that makes variance measurement meaningful. Baselines can legitimately change — but only through approved change control, typically for approved scope changes, not to hide slippage. Continuous tracking is what actuals do, not baselines.

title: Common ITTO Patterns: EEFs, OPAs, Plans, and Documents

eco_mappings.task: Task 1: Develop an integrated project management plan and plan delivery

learning_objectives: Define Enterprise Environmental Factors (EEFs) and Organizational Process Assets (OPAs).
Recognize the repeating ITTO (input-tool/technique-output) pattern across processes.
Distinguish the project management plan from project documents as two different categories of output.

sections.title: The ITTO Pattern
sections.content: Nearly every PMBOK process follows the same shape: **Inputs** (what you need to start), **Tools and Techniques** (how you do the work), and **Outputs** (what you produce). Recognizing this repeating pattern is more valuable than memorizing every process's specific ITTO list.

sections.title: Enterprise Environmental Factors (EEFs)
sections.content: **EEFs** are conditions, not under the project team's direct control, that influence, constrain, or direct the project — organizational culture, market conditions, government regulations, infrastructure, and similar external or internal environmental factors.

sections.title: Organizational Process Assets (OPAs)
sections.content: **OPAs** are the organization's own accumulated assets — templates, historical information, lessons learned repositories, policies, and procedures — available to help plan and execute the project.

sections.title: Plans vs. Documents
sections.content: The **project management plan** describes how the project will be managed. **Project documents** (like the risk register, issue log, or stakeholder register) are working records used throughout execution but aren't themselves part of the formal management plan.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: When a scenario names a specific factor (a new regulation, a company template), classifying it correctly as an EEF or OPA is often the real test — regulations and market conditions are EEFs; templates and lessons-learned repositories are OPAs.

exam_traps: Confusing EEFs (external/internal conditions outside the team's control) with OPAs (the organization's own accumulated assets).
Assuming ITTOs must be memorized process-by-process rather than recognized as a repeating input-tool-output pattern.
Treating project documents as part of the formal management plan rather than separate working records.

flashcards.definition: A condition outside the project team's direct control that influences, constrains, or directs the project.
An organization's own accumulated assets — templates, historical data, lessons learned — available to support the project.

knowledge_checks.question: A new government regulation constrains how the project may store customer data. How is this classified?
options: An OPA, since the organization has compliance templates for it
An EEF — a condition outside the team's control that constrains the project
A project document, since it must be recorded in the compliance plan
An OPA, because the organization is the one that must implement it
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: An EEF — a condition outside the team's control that constrains the project
knowledge_checks.explanation: Regulations, market conditions, and organizational culture are Enterprise Environmental Factors: conditions that influence or constrain the project from outside the team's control. OPAs are the organization's own assets (templates, procedures, lessons learned); who implements compliance doesn't change the regulation's classification.

knowledge_checks.question: The project manager pulls a lessons-learned repository and an estimating template from the company's process library. What are these?
options: EEFs — internal environmental factors influencing the project
Project documents created by the current project
OPAs — the organization's accumulated process assets
Components of the project management plan
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: OPAs — the organization's accumulated process assets
knowledge_checks.explanation: Templates, historical information, and lessons-learned repositories are Organizational Process Assets — accumulated organizational property available to help the project. They weren't created by the current project (so not its project documents) and they describe help available, not conditions imposed (so not EEFs).

knowledge_checks.question: Which of these is a project document rather than part of the project management plan?
options: The risk register
The scope management plan
The cost management plan
The communications management plan
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The risk register
knowledge_checks.explanation: The risk register is a working record used during execution — a project document. The scope, cost, and communications management plans are subsidiary plans inside the integrated project management plan, which describes how the project is managed rather than recording day-to-day working state.

title: Work Performance Data, Information, and Reports
learning_objectives: Define work performance data, work performance information, and work performance reports.
Explain the escalating hierarchy from raw data to contextualized information to formatted reports.
Describe how this hierarchy supports the Monitoring and Controlling focus area.

sections.title: An Escalating Hierarchy
sections.content: Project status flows through three increasingly processed stages, and confusing them is a common exam trap.

sections.title: Work Performance Data
sections.content: **Work performance data** is the raw, unanalyzed observations and measurements collected during execution — hours worked, defects found, costs incurred. It's not yet interpreted.

sections.title: Work Performance Information
sections.content: **Work performance information** is the data after it's been analyzed and put in context — comparing actual to planned, calculating variances, identifying trends. This is what actually informs decisions.

sections.title: Work Performance Reports
sections.content: **Work performance reports** are the physical or electronic representation of performance information, compiled to support decision-making, raise issues, or communicate status to stakeholders.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: A scenario describing raw numbers being collected is testing 'data.' A scenario describing analysis, variance, or trend identification is testing 'information.' A scenario describing something being formatted and distributed to stakeholders is testing 'reports.'

exam_traps: Treating data, information, and reports as interchangeable terms rather than a defined hierarchy.
Assuming raw data alone is sufficient for a status update without analysis or context.
Confusing 'information' (analyzed) with 'reports' (formatted and distributed) — information feeds into reports, it isn't the same artifact.

flashcards.definition: Raw, unanalyzed observations and measurements collected during project execution.
Performance data after analysis and contextualization, such as calculated variances or trends.
The compiled, formatted representation of performance information used for decision-making and communication.

knowledge_checks.question: 'The team logged 412 hours and found 9 defects this week.' In the work performance hierarchy, what is this?
options: Work performance information, since it was formally collected
A work performance report, since it was written down
An organizational process asset, since it will be archived
Work performance data — raw, unanalyzed measurements
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Work performance data — raw, unanalyzed measurements
knowledge_checks.explanation: Raw observations with no analysis or context are work performance data. It becomes information only once analyzed against the plan, and a report only once formatted for distribution. Collection method and eventual archiving don't change its place in the hierarchy.

knowledge_checks.question: Comparing those logged hours against the plan reveals a 15% adverse variance and a worsening three-week trend. What has the data become?
options: It remains raw data until leadership has formally reviewed and accepted it
Work performance information — analyzed and placed in planning context
A work performance report, since a numeric variance has been calculated
A formal revision to the schedule baseline reflecting the adverse trend
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Work performance information — analyzed and placed in planning context
knowledge_checks.explanation: Analysis against the plan — variances, trends, comparisons — turns raw data into work performance information, which is what actually informs decisions. Leadership review isn't the transformation step, formatting for distribution is what makes a report, and baselines change only through change control.

knowledge_checks.question: What distinguishes a work performance report from work performance information?
options: A report formats information for distribution and decision-making
A report contains the raw numbers while information is the formatted product
Reports come before information in the processing hierarchy
Only reports are allowed to contain variance calculations
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: A report formats information for distribution and decision-making
knowledge_checks.explanation: The hierarchy runs data → information → reports: the report is the packaged representation of analyzed information, compiled for stakeholders and decisions. The distractors invert the sequence or invent restrictions on where variances may appear.

title: PMO Types and Organizational Structures

eco_mappings.task: Task 1: Define and establish project governance

learning_objectives: Define a PMO and describe its three common types.
Explain how organizational structure affects project manager authority.
Distinguish weak, balanced, and strong matrix structures.

sections.title: What a PMO Does
sections.content: A **Project Management Office (PMO)** is an organizational structure that standardizes project-related governance and facilitates the sharing of resources, methodologies, tools, and techniques across projects.

sections.title: Three PMO Types
sections.content:
- **Supportive** — advisory role, provides templates and best practices, low control
- **Controlling** — requires compliance with specific frameworks or methodologies, moderate control
- **Directive** — takes direct control of projects, providing project management experience and resources, high control

sections.title: Organizational Structures and PM Authority
sections.content: A project manager's authority varies significantly by structure: in a **functional** structure, authority is low and resources report to functional managers; in a **projectized** structure, authority is high and resources report directly to the project manager; a **matrix** structure sits in between, with weak, balanced, and strong variants reflecting increasing project manager authority.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: A scenario naming a specific structure or PMO type is testing whether you know how much real authority the project manager holds in that context — this frequently determines whether the 'right' answer involves the project manager deciding directly or negotiating with a functional manager.

exam_traps: Assuming all PMOs have direct control over projects — a supportive PMO's role is largely advisory.
Confusing matrix strength (weak/balanced/strong) with the project manager's actual formal authority, which varies significantly across that spectrum.
Assuming a projectized structure has no PMO involvement — a PMO can still exist to standardize practice even where the PM holds high authority.

flashcards.definition: A PMO type that takes direct control of projects, providing project management experience and resources.
An organizational structure where the project manager holds significant authority, though resources still have a functional reporting line.

knowledge_checks.question: A PMO provides templates and best practices to project teams but cannot require any project to follow them. Which PMO type is this?
options: Controlling
Supportive
Directive
Projectized
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Supportive
knowledge_checks.explanation: A supportive PMO plays an advisory role with low control — it offers assets but can't mandate their use. A controlling PMO requires compliance with frameworks, a directive PMO runs projects directly, and 'projectized' is an organizational structure, not a PMO type.

knowledge_checks.question: In which organizational structure does the project manager hold the least formal authority?
options: Projectized
Strong matrix
Functional
Balanced matrix
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Functional
knowledge_checks.explanation: In a functional structure, resources report to functional managers and the project manager's authority is minimal. Authority rises through weak, balanced, and strong matrix forms and peaks in a projectized structure, where resources report directly to the project manager.

knowledge_checks.question: In a weak matrix organization, a functional manager keeps pulling a shared resource onto department work. Why does the structure matter to how the project manager responds?
options: It does not matter — resource decisions work identically in every structure
The resource is entitled to choose whichever manager they prefer working for
The PM can simply order the resource to return to project work immediately
Low formal authority points to negotiating or escalating, not directing
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: Low formal authority points to negotiating or escalating, not directing
knowledge_checks.explanation: In a weak matrix the functional manager holds most of the authority, so the effective play is negotiation with that manager and escalation through the sponsor if needed — not issuing orders the structure doesn't back. The exam frequently keys the 'right' action to how much authority the described structure actually gives the PM.

title: Project vs. Product Management
learning_objectives: Distinguish project manager responsibilities from product manager responsibilities.
Explain how product thinking (ongoing value delivery) differs from project thinking (bounded deliverable).
Describe why product-centric organizations blend both roles.

sections.title: Two Related but Different Roles
sections.content: A **project manager** and a **product manager** are complementary roles with different time horizons, and confusing them is a common exam trap.

sections.title: Project Manager Focus
sections.content: The project manager is responsible for delivering a specific, bounded outcome within defined constraints — scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, risk. Their responsibility typically ends when the project closes.

sections.title: Product Manager Focus
sections.content: The product manager is responsible for a product's ongoing vision, strategy, and value delivery across its entire life cycle, which continues well beyond any single project. They own the 'why' behind what gets built, informed by market and user needs.

sections.title: Where They Intersect
sections.content: In product-centric and agile organizations, these roles work closely together: the product manager (often via a product owner role) prioritizes what has value, while the project manager (or equivalent delivery lead) ensures the work is planned and executed effectively.

sections.title: Exam Perspective
sections.content: A scenario testing whether a decision belongs to 'the project manager' or 'the product owner/manager' is usually testing whether you understand this project-bounded versus product-lifecycle distinction, not just organizational titles.

exam_traps: Assuming a project manager's responsibility extends to product vision — that's typically the product manager's domain.
Assuming these roles are interchangeable rather than complementary with different time horizons.
Forgetting that a product's life cycle continues after any single project delivering into it has closed.

flashcards.definition: Owns a product's ongoing vision, strategy, and value delivery across its entire life cycle.
The full span of a product's existence, continuing beyond any single project delivering into it.

knowledge_checks.question: Who owns a product's ongoing vision, strategy, and value delivery beyond any single project?
options: The project manager
The sponsor
The product manager
The PMO director
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The product manager
knowledge_checks.explanation: The product manager owns the product across its whole life cycle — the 'why' behind what gets built. The project manager delivers bounded outcomes within constraints, the sponsor champions and funds, and a PMO standardizes practice; none of them owns ongoing product vision.

knowledge_checks.question: A project delivering a major release into a product closes successfully. What happens to responsibility for the product's value?
options: It ends completely, since the project met every one of its constraints
It continues with the product manager across the product life cycle
It transfers permanently to the steering committee going forward
It pauses until the next related project is formally chartered
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: It continues with the product manager across the product life cycle
knowledge_checks.explanation: Product value responsibility outlives every individual project: the product manager keeps steering vision and value after the project team disbands. Project closure ends the project's accountability for its bounded deliverable, not the product's ongoing life.

knowledge_checks.question: In an agile team, who decides what is most valuable to build next, and who ensures the work is planned and executed effectively?
options: The product owner prioritizes value; the delivery lead ensures execution
The project manager prioritizes value; the product owner manages execution
The sponsor performs both roles personally to avoid any conflict of interest
The team votes on both questions together at the start of each new sprint
knowledge_checks.correct_answer: The product owner prioritizes value; the delivery lead ensures execution
knowledge_checks.explanation: Value prioritization belongs to the product owner (the product-management side); planning and execution discipline belongs to the delivery lead or project manager. Reversing the roles is the classic trap, and neither sponsor fiat nor team voting describes how these complementary accountabilities actually divide.
